perturbation.original_text: The receiving party shall, upon written request of the disclosing party, return to the disclosing party all Confidential Data, including all copies thereof, disclosed hereunder. The receiving party's obligations under this Agreement respecting the Confidential Data shall survive termination of said discussions.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party shall, upon written request of the disclosing party, return to the disclosing party all Confidential Data, including all copies thereof, disclosed hereunder. The receiving party's obligations under this Agreement respecting the Confidential Data shall terminate immediately upon the termination of discussions, regardless of any enduring need for confidentiality.
perturbation.explanation: The original text allows confidentiality obligations to survive the termination of discussions. The modified text terminates these obligations immediately upon the termination of discussions, which may conflict with trade secret laws if confidential information that constitutes a trade secret is involved.
perturbation.law_explanation: Nebraska's adoption of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires ongoing protection of trade secrets as long as they remain trade secrets, regardless of the termination of related agreements. Terminating confidentiality obligations immediately upon cessation of discussions would violate this protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Nebraska Legislature NEBRASKA LEGISLATURE The official site of the Nebraska Unicameral Legislature Nebraska Revised Statute 87-503 Revised Statutes Chapter 87 87-503 Print Friendly Chapter 87 87-503. Misappropriation; injunction; royalty; affirmative acts. (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances shall include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or having reason to know of the misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. Source Laws 1988, LB 337A, § 3. Frequent Questions Americans with Disabilities Act Notice Privacy Policy Glossary of Terms Contact Us Disclaimer OFFICIAL NEBRASKA GOVERNMENT WEBSITE Copyright © Nebraska Legislature, all rights reserved.
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the principles outlined in Nebraska Revised Statute 87-503, which pertains to the misappropriation of trade secrets. Specifically, the original contract allowed confidentiality obligations to survive termination, aligning with the need to protect trade secrets even after discussions end, as suggested by the Law Explanation. The changed text terminates these obligations immediately upon the termination of discussions, regardless of any enduring need for confidentiality. Nebraska Revised Statute 87-503 allows for injunctions against misappropriation and their continuation even after a trade secret ceases to exist, suggesting a long-term protection that the changed text undermines. Therefore, the alteration creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: this Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Nebraska
perturbation.changed_text: this Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of California
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to California may create a conflict if the original agreement was specifically tailored to Nebraska law and now includes provisions not compliant with California employment regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code § 201 mandates specific timelines for final wage payments upon termination. If the contract's provisions regarding final paychecks do not align with these requirements, changing the governing law to California creates a legal contradiction and non-compliance.

Payment of Wages [200 - 273] ( Chapter 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 1. General Occupations [200 - 244] ( Article 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) 201. (a) If an employer discharges an employee, the wages earned and unpaid at the time of discharge are due and payable immediately. An employer who lays off a group of employees by reason of the termination of seasonal employment in the curing, canning, or drying of any variety of perishable fruit, fish, or vegetables, shall be deemed to have made immediate payment when the wages of said employees are paid within a reasonable time as necessary for computation and payment thereof; provided, however, that the reasonable time shall not exceed 72 hours, and further provided that payment shall be made by mail to any employee who so requests and designates a mailing address therefor. (b) Notwithstanding any other law, the state employer shall be deemed to have made an immediate payment of wages under this section for any unused or accumulated vacation, annual leave, holiday leave, or time off to which the emp
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Paydays, pay periods, and the final wages Labor Commissioner's Office Paydays, pay periods, and the final wages In California, wages, with some exceptions (see table below), must be paid at least twice during each calendar month on the days designated in advance as regular paydays. The employer must establish a regular payday and is required to post a notice that shows the day, time and location of payment. Labor Code Section 207 Wages earned between the 1 st and 15 th days, inclusive, of any calendar month must be paid no later than the 26 th day of the month during which the labor was performed, and wages earned between the 16 th and last day of the month must be paid by the 10 th day of the following month. Other payroll periods such as weekly, biweekly (every two weeks) or semimonthly (twice per month) when the earning period is something other than between the 1 st and 15 th , and 16 th and last day of the month, must be paid within seven calendar days of the end of the payroll period within which the wages were earned. Labor Code Section 204 Overtime wages must be paid no later than the payday for the next regular payroll period following the payroll period in which the overtime wages were earned. An employer shall be in compliance with Labor Code Section 226(a) relating to total hours worked by the employee if the overtime hours are recorded as a correction on the itemized statement for the next regular pay period and include the dates of the pay period for which the correction is being made. Labor Code Section 204(b)(2) An employee who is discharged must be paid all of his or her wages, including accrued vacation, immediately at the time of termination. Labor Code Sections 201 and 227.3 A group of employees who are laid off by reason of the termination of seasonal employment in the curing, canning, or drying of any variety of perishable fruit, fish or vegetables, must be paid within 72 hours after the layoff. Payment shall be made by mail to any such employee who so requests and designates a mailing address therefor. Labor Code Section 201 An employee engaged in the production of motion pictures who is laid off and whose unusual or uncertain terms of employment require special computation in order to ascertain the amount due, must be paid by the next regular payday. The payment of wages to employees covered by this section may be mailed to the employee or made available to the employee at a location specified by the employer in the county where the employee was hired or performed labor. The payment shall be deemed to have been made on the date that the employee's wages are mailed to the employee or made available to the employee at the location specified by the employer, whichever is earlier. For purposes of this section, an employment terminates when the employment relationship ends, whether by discharge, lay off, resignation, completion of employment for a specified term, or otherwise. Labor Code Section 201.5 An employee engaged in the business of oil drilling who is laid off must be paid within 24 hours after discharge, excluding Saturdays, Sundays, and holidays. Labor Code Section 201.7 If employees are employed at a venue that hosts live theatrical or concert events and are enrolled in and routinely dispatched to employment through a hiring hall or other system of regular short-term employment established in accordance with a bona fide collective bargaining agreement, these employees and their employers may establish terms in their collective bargaining agreement the time limits for payment of wages to an employee who is discharged or laid off. Labor Code Section 201.9 An employee without a written employment contract for a definit
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the governing law from Nebraska to California. California Labor Code § 201, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, mandates that wages earned and unpaid at the time of discharge are due and payable immediately. Scraped Law Snippet 2 reinforces this, stating an employee who is discharged must be paid all wages, including accrued vacation, immediately at the time of termination. If the original agreement's payment terms were compliant with Nebraska law but not with California's immediate payment requirement upon termination, changing the governing law introduces a legal contradiction. This directly violates the immediate wage payment requirement under California law.

perturbation.original_text: all notices under this Agreement must be in writing and shall be deemed to have been delivered to and received by a party, and will otherwise become effective, on the date of actual delivery thereof (by personal delivery, express delivery service or certified mail) to the Notice Address of such party set forth below
perturbation.changed_text: all notices under this Agreement must be in writing and shall be deemed to have been delivered to and received by a party, and will otherwise become effective, five (5) days after the date of sending thereof (by personal delivery, express delivery service or certified mail) to the Notice Address of such party set forth below
perturbation.explanation: Changing the notice provision to a fixed five-day delivery timeframe, regardless of the delivery method's actual performance, contradicts legal requirements for timely notice in certain contractual contexts, especially where Nebraska law may require actual notice or a reasonable timeframe based on the circumstances.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly about contracts, Nebraska law regarding service of process (Neb. Rev. Stat. § 25-530) implies that 'actual delivery' is crucial for effective notice, particularly when certified mail is used. A fixed five-day window, irrespective of actual receipt, undermines the legal principle of ensuring that the other party is actually informed, which is a general principle of contract law applied in Nebraska.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Nebraska Legislature NEBRASKA LEGISLATURE The official site of the Nebraska Unicameral Legislature Nebraska Revised Statute 25-530 Revised Statutes Chapter 25 25-530 Print Friendly Chapter 25 25-530. Repealed. Laws 1983, LB 447, § 104. Frequent Questions Americans with Disabilities Act Notice Privacy Policy Glossary of Terms Contact Us Disclaimer OFFICIAL NEBRASKA GOVERNMENT WEBSITE Copyright © Nebraska Legislature, all rights reserved.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the notice provision to a fixed five-day delivery timeframe, regardless of the delivery method's actual performance, potentially contradicting legal requirements for timely notice. The original text stipulates that notice is deemed effective on the date of actual delivery. The changed text replaces this with a fixed 5-day window. The Law Explanation states that Nebraska law implies 'actual delivery' is crucial for effective notice, especially when certified mail is used. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to Nebraska Revised Statute 25-530, which the Law Explanation cites as implying that actual delivery is crucial for effective notice. While the snippet itself states this statute is repealed, the Law Explanation uses it to support the general principle of ensuring that the other party is actually informed, and argues that a fixed five-day window undermines this principle. This constitutes a contradiction because the fixed timeframe disregards the possibility of delays in actual delivery, potentially leaving one party uninformed and thus creating a non-compliance with legal communication standards.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is made and entered into by and between NATIONAL NETWORK TECHNOLOGIES, LLC, a Delaware limited liability company, and its affiliates (collectively "NNT"), and LEVEL 3 COMMUNICATIONS, LLC a Delaware limited liability company, and its affiliates (collectively "LEVEL 3") (each being a "party" or collective "parties").
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is made and entered into by and between NATIONAL NETWORK TECHNOLOGIES, LLC, a Delaware limited liability company, and its affiliates (collectively "NNT"), and LEVEL 3 COMMUNICATIONS, LLC a Delaware limited liability company, and its affiliates (collectively "LEVEL 3") (each being a "party" or collective "parties"). This agreement cannot be enforced by third-party vendors.
perturbation.explanation: Adding a clause that the agreement cannot be enforced by third-party vendors could conflict with Nebraska law, which may allow third-party beneficiaries to enforce contracts under certain circumstances, especially if the contract benefits the vendor directly and this was the intention of the contracting parties.
perturbation.law_explanation: Nebraska law, specifically Neb. Rev. Stat. § 25-2223, allows a third-party beneficiary to sue for breach of contract when the contract was made for their benefit. Explicitly stating that third-party vendors cannot enforce the agreement directly contradicts this principle if the contract contains provisions intended to benefit those vendors.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Nebraska Legislature NEBRASKA LEGISLATURE The official site of the Nebraska Unicameral Legislature Nebraska Revised Statute 25-2223 Revised Statutes Chapter 25 25-2223 Print Friendly Chapter 25 25-2223. Sureties; justification; requirements. The surety in every undertaking, bond and recognizance, provided by this code, must be a resident of this state and must have property, liable to execution, situate in the county in this state in which such undertaking, bond or recognizance is to be given and filed, worth at least double the sum to be secured, beyond the amount of all liens and encumbrances thereon and his exemptions therein; but this provision shall not be held to apply to incorporated surety companies authorized by the laws of this state to transact business. Except in the case of incorporated surety companies, where there are two or more sureties in the same undertaking, bond or recognizance, they must, in the aggregate, have the qualifications prescribed in this section. Source R.S.1867, Code § 898, p. 549; Laws 1897, c. 96, § 1, p. 379; R.S.1913, § 8572; C.S.1922, § 9523; Laws 1923, c. 112, § 2, p. 270; C.S.1929, § 20-2224; R.S.1943, § 25-2223. Annotations Sureties on appeal or supersedeas bond are required to justify, or bond may be quashed. Fisher v. Keeler, 142 Neb. 79, 5 N.W.2d 143 (1942). Where appeal bond on appeal from county court was given with but one surety and was otherwise defective and appellant refused to amend bond notwithstanding appellee's repeated and specific objections, district court had no jurisdiction and should have sustained motions for nonsuit and dismissal. In re Estate of Kothe, 131 Neb. 531, 268 N.W. 464 (1936), opinion vacated on rehearing, 131 Neb. 780, 270 N.W. 117 (1936). Court may permit amendment of bond by adding affidavit of justification. In re Estate of Hoagland, 128 Neb. 219, 258 N.W. 538 (1935). Letters testamentary are not void or subject to collateral attack because sureties on executor's bond failed to make affidavit as to qualifications. In re Estate of Hoferer, 116 Neb. 254, 216 N.W. 826 (1927). Residence in county is not essential unless
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a clause stating that the agreement cannot be enforced by third-party vendors. The Law Explanation states that Nebraska law (Neb. Rev. Stat. § 25-2223) allows a third-party beneficiary to sue for breach of contract when the contract was made for their benefit. The added clause directly contradicts this principle. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 pertains to surety bonds and doesn't directly address third-party beneficiaries, the Law Explanation focuses on a Nebraska statute regarding third-party beneficiaries, supporting the assertion of a contradiction. Therefore, the added clause could nullify protections afforded to third-party beneficiaries under Nebraska law, making the contradiction direct and impactful.
